steps.title: Compare the study claims
steps.content: Text 1 presents Kolvereid and Moen's study as persuasive evidence: participants showed strong intentions to become entrepreneurs as a result of entrepreneurial training. Text 2 accepts that the study reported positive results, but it criticizes the method because data were collected only after training, not before. Without pre-training data, the researchers could not show whether participants already had those intentions before the program. That means Text 2 doubts whether the training caused the reported results.

steps.title: Eliminate unsupported questions
steps.content: Choice A asks whether training costs are offset by benefits, a topic neither text mentions. Choice C asks whether Kolvereid and Moen reported strong entrepreneurial intentions; Text 2 does not deny that they reported those results. It disputes what those results prove. Choice D asks about different types of entrepreneurial training programs, but neither text compares program types or outcomes across programs. Choice B asks whether the study persuasively showed that its results were attributable to the training participants received, which is exactly where the texts disagree. The answer is B.
